data.title: Sirius XM's Weird Space Rally Explained
data.description:
A Likely Outperformer

Sirius XM Holdings Inc. (SIRI) has been an extraordinarily satisfying holding in recent weeks, and although it is up 40% YTD, it is still very cheap, trading for just 8.45x forward EPS and sporting a 4.13% dividend yield.

The company has just concluded a multi-year investment cycle, is almost certain to deliver $1.35 billion of FCF in 2026 (15.3% FCF yield), and will resume substantial buybacks next year, when debt levels will have been reduced thanks to those FCF inflows.

Looking Like A Value Trap

That said, the chart is ugly. Even after its recent recovery, the stock has more than halved from its usual levels a few years ago and has burnt many longs by flip-flopping on its streaming strategy, (slowly) losing subscribers, and overall, the company looks like just another legacy media loser and a typical value trap.

Many short-lived rallies have come on its way down and lured in investors; there have been occasional short squeezes, but ultimately the stock has always resumed its downward trajectory.

Why Should This Rally Be Different?

Last year, when I called the bottom for the stock, I wrote about the increasing investor interest in Sirius XM’s spectrum assets:

Since Sirius XM (roughly speaking) owns about twice as much spectrum as it needs, there is a huge monetization opportunity. While an outright sale is complicated, probably not even smart to do (as it would create some technical and regulatory problems), the fastest way to make money from this asset is through third-party partnerships.

It looks like we will soon find out how such partnerships can unfold and add to Sirius XM’s bottom line.

Two weeks ago, right before the stock added 20% in a few days, the Federal Register released a Proposed Rule, “Spectrum Abundance for Weird Space Stuff,” the only weird detail of which is the title. Absent negative comments, the new rule will become effective on 5/11/26. Otherwise, by 6/8/26, reply comments are due.

In a lengthy document, the FCC (Federal Communications Commission) explains why it wants to expand Sirius XM’s rights concerning its spectrum to allow for additional uses for telemetry, tracking, and command (TT&C) functions that are essential for operating emergent spacecraft. ‘‘Emergent space operations’’ are defined as “those spacecraft or commercial operations in space that use radio spectrum for control of, or communications with, a spacecraft, but which are not communications satellites.”

The agency justifies its intervention by the current shortage of usable and readily accessible spectrum for the mentioned TT&C functions. Additionally, the document proposes to add a secondary allocation for SOS (Space Operation Service) in spectrum frequency bands such as Sirius XM's, which are used below their capacity in certain geographic areas. Finally, the new rule would allow the leasing of such spectrum to earth station licensees to provide SOS in connection with emergent spacecraft.

As the document explains,

Once the province of science fiction, American companies are now upgrading, relocating, and servicing satellites; manufacturing pharmaceuticals in space; building private inhabitable spacecraft; and conducting private robotic missions to the surface of the Moon. Emergent space operations like these depend on the use of radiocommunications for their spacecraft. … Spectrum is a critical component of all space operations. Even for spacecraft that do not provide radiocommunications services to the public, reliable spectrum access is mandatory … to control spacecraft in orbit. American innovators, however, currently face an acute shortage of usable and readily accessible spectrum for TT&C, and that spectrum crunch threatens to delay—or even prevent— the growth of domestic space technologies and jeopardize U.S. leadership in the booming global space economy. … For one, spectrum for TT&C is largely unavailable on a standalone basis, absent a separately authorized radiocommunications service. Emergent space innovations like inhabitable spacecraft and orbital laboratories, however, do not typically provide a separately authorized radiocommunications service. For another, the spectrum bands where TT&C may be available on a standalone basis tend to be congested and occupied primarily by Federal users.

Importantly, there is currently no domestic or international radiocommunication service specifically dedicated for use by emergent space operations. Consequently, the agency proposes

to add a secondary allocation for SOS (Earth-to-space) in the 2320–2345 MHz band in the non-Federal column of the United States Table, and to permit the existing exclusive licensee within this frequency band in the United States, SiriusXM, to lease portions of its spectrum to earth station licensees for command of spacecraft that do not otherwise provide radiocommunication services to the public. … Because TT&C functions are not always transmitting and employ narrowband signals, often 100 kilohertz in bandwidth or less, we anticipate that a large number of TT&C communications can be accommodated in the 25 megahertz of the 2320–2345 MHz band, particularly if multiple earth station uplink sites can be coordinated with SiriusXM.

Finally, the agency basically intends to transform Sirius XM into some sort of gatekeeper for emerging space operations and explicitly allows Sirius XM to be compensated for such efforts:

As proposed above, any earth station licensee that seeks to operate in the 2320–2345 MHz band would have to certify that its proposed operations have been coordinated with and approved by SiriusXM in order to avoid harmful interference to SiriusXM’s current and potential operations. … Such coordination is likely to require expenditure of resources by SiriusXM, or the forgoing of future rights to operate in certain areas, for the benefit of third party spectrum users. As such, we tentatively conclude it is reasonable for SiriusXM to require financial compensation for the expenditures under these conditions.

What Is It All Worth?

It is impossible to know the earnings potential from the proposed spectrum licensing.

Typically, spectrum licensing deals are priced based on population coverage, MHz, and technical specifics of the spectrum involved. Prices range from a few cents per MHz/POP to $2 per MHz/POP. For example, 1 MHz times 300 million people times $2 would result in an annual licensing fee of $600m.

That said, the spectrum licensing market is set to grow strongly over the next decade, as spectrum uses increase while spectrum availability is scarce. This means licensing fees are very likely to increase strongly over time.

In its recent 10-K, Sirius XM states a carrying value for its spectrum licenses of $8.6 billion.

The recent transaction between AT&T Inc. (T) and EchoStar Corporation (SATS) involved twice as much spectrum as Sirius XM’s 25 MHz and had a price tag of $23 billion. The other EchoStar transaction with SpaceX also involved 50 MHz and cost $19 billion. However, this can only serve as a rough goalpost, as the value of spectrum is determined by many different factors.

Clearly, the $8.6 billion carrying value was already justified by the current resulting earning power of the asset. Sirius XM will probably extract some $1.6 billion of unlevered FCF from its activities in 2026, and its enterprise value is just 11.6 times as much at $18.6 billion.

Any additional dollar of earnings should increment its valuation—even more so as it has been treated as a dying business.

Even without knowing exactly how much the potential new “space gatekeeper” business might add to the bottom line, probably the main takeaway for investors from this new regulatory proposal is that Sirius XM has growth options. It is not dying.

By the way, yesterday's announcement of an exclusive advertising partnership with YouTube highlights that industry experts perfectly recognize Sirius XM's market-leading position. The agreement makes SiriusXM Media the exclusive advertising representative of YouTube audio advertising inventory in the U.S.

Once at least some growth is factored in, $10 billion of debt is not an issue anymore. Shorts will cover. Cash will be available for buybacks and even higher dividends instead of debt reduction. A higher stock market valuation might lead to index inclusion and forced index fund buying.

Moreover, Sirius XM could be acquired after September 2026, when some provisions resulting from the Liberty Media merger expire.

For a long time, Sirius XM stock has suffered from a slowly declining business, which casts a long shadow over its many interesting assets. Even the tiniest bit of growth can suddenly and completely invert the picture.

data.title: BUZZ Investing: April Rebound Calms A March Washout
data.description:
U.S. equities sold off sharply through late March on geopolitical tensions and Fed caution, then rebounded in early April on stronger-than-expected jobs data and easing macro fears.

ImmunityBio (IBRX) entered at the maximum 3% weight on a social sentiment surge around its cancer immunotherapy ANKTIVA, joined by four crypto-related equities signaling a broad inflection in digital asset sentiment.

U.S. equity markets experienced continued volatility and a broad-based defensive retrenchment through much of the recent period between selection dates (March 12, 2026 – April 9, 2026, the “Period”), before staging a sharp recovery in the opening days of April that lifted the major indices back into positive territory. The opening phase extended the risk-off dynamics that had taken hold in early March, with the U.S.-Israel-Iran conflict showing no immediate signs of resolution. Elevated energy prices, still reflecting earlier disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz, kept inflation concerns elevated, while the Federal Reserve’s March 18 meeting delivered little reassurance, holding the target upper bound of its policy rate at 3.75 percent and maintaining a cautious stance amid unresolved macro crosscurrents.

Investor positioning shifted decisively away from long-duration growth and AI-related equities, with software, semiconductor, and digital infrastructure names among the hardest hit as valuation support eroded and correlations spiked. By late March the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) had slipped into correction territory, and both the S&P 500 and Nasdaq had recorded intra-Period drawdowns of approximately 5 percent. Against this backdrop, the BUZZ NextGen AI US Sentiment Leaders Index (the “BUZZ Index”) proved especially vulnerable, selling off more than 10 percent during the Period as its higher-beta constituents participated disproportionately in the liquidation phase.

The tone improved materially in the first nine days of April, though the macro backdrop had not fully normalized. Incremental de-escalation signals in the geopolitical narrative, bolstered by improving negotiation headlines out of Washington and the region, helped ease tail risks and supported a rebound in risk appetite. This was reinforced by a stronger-than-expected March nonfarm payrolls report, which showed an increase of 178,000 jobs and the unemployment rate edging down to 4.3 percent, countering late-March fears of rapid economic deterioration. Investors also looked through a hotter March inflation print, interpreting it as transitory in light of resilient underlying demand. At the same time, fresh evidence of durable commercial traction across parts of the AI complex, particularly in enterprise applications and infrastructure, helped restore selective confidence in growth-oriented leadership.

By April 9, the S&P 500 (SP500) and Nasdaq Composite recovered sufficiently to finish the Period in positive territory, up 2.4 percent and 2.3 percent, respectively. The BUZZ Index lagged the broader rebound, closing down 2.7 percent, reflecting the fact that many of its constituents had borne the brunt of the earlier drawdown and only partially recouped losses as sentiment stabilized. While the Period ended on a constructive note, underlying macro crosscurrents including geopolitical uncertainty, inflation vigilance, and Fed caution, remained in place, leaving the market’s conviction still somewhat tentative.

The BUZZ Index returned -6.22% during the month of March compared to a return of -4.98% for the S&P 500 Index during the same period. Year-to-date, the BUZZ Index lags the S&P 500 with returns of -11.21% and -4.33%, respectively, as of the end of March.

Nebius, Intel, and AMD Lead BUZZ Gains on AI Infrastructure Momentum

Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS) was the leading contributor to BUZZ Index performance during the Period, marking its second consecutive appearance among the top contributors. The continued strength reflected sustained investor interest in companies viewed as credible beneficiaries of the AI infrastructure buildout, particularly where capital access, contracted demand, and expansion plans appeared tangible. Following the Nvidia (NVDA) investment and strategic validation discussed in the prior Period, Nebius remained well supported as investors absorbed its March agreement to provide Meta with up to $27 billion of AI computing capacity and its subsequent announcement of a 310-megawatt data center project in Finland, which would rank among Europe’s largest. Together, those developments reinforced the view that Nebius is evolving into a more scaled infrastructure platform with visible growth potential.

Intel Corporation (INTC) and Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) were also among the leading contributors during the Period, as investors rotated back into semiconductor names viewed as beneficiaries of sustained AI infrastructure spending. Intel’s advance was supported by several company-specific developments late in the Period, including its agreement to repurchase Apollo’s stake in its Ireland fabrication facility, its participation in Elon Musk’s Terafab initiative, and an expanded partnership with Google focused on AI and cloud infrastructure. AMD also participated in the rebound as confidence improved around the durability of AI-related capital spending and the company’s position within that ecosystem. During the Period, AMD announced an expanded strategic partnership with Samsung (SSNLF) around AI memory and manufacturing, while continued investor focus on its large-scale supply relationships with customers such as Meta (META) supported the broader view that AMD remains one of the few scaled alternatives in high-performance computing.

Super Micro and Hims & Hers Health Lead BUZZ Declines on Company-Specific Setbacks

Super Micro Computer, Inc. (SMCI) was the largest detractor from BUZZ Index performance during the Period, declining sharply after U.S. prosecutors charged the company’s co-founder, Yih-Shyan Liaw, along with two others, in connection with an alleged scheme to smuggle American-made AI servers containing restricted Nvidia chips into China in violation of U.S. export controls. Although Super Micro was not itself charged, the development revived broader concerns around governance, compliance, and customer trust at a company that had already faced heightened scrutiny over internal controls and accounting matters. The company responded by placing implicated individuals on leave, removing Liaw from the board, elevating a new chief compliance officer, and later launching an independent probe, but those steps did little to stabilize sentiment during the Period. The severity of the stock’s decline reflected not only the legal overhang, but also investor concern that the episode could disrupt commercial momentum or invite further regulatory scrutiny at a time when Super Micro had been trying to re-establish credibility as a key beneficiary of AI infrastructure spending.

Hims & Hers Health, Inc. (HIMS) was another notable detractor during the Period as investor skepticism continued to build around the durability and profitability of its weight-loss business. The stock remained under pressure as analysts reassessed the company’s 2026 earnings outlook considering an expected transition away from lower-cost compounded GLP-1 offerings toward branded products, a shift viewed as likely to compress margins and complicate subscriber conversion. That concern came against an already fragile backdrop following the FDA’s earlier crackdown on copycat GLP-1 products and growing questions around whether Hims can diversify quickly enough to offset pressure on its U.S. weight-loss franchise. While management has argued that the business can adapt and broaden beyond GLP-1s over time, the market appeared unconvinced during the Period, particularly as competition intensified and investors weighed the risk that growth may slow more sharply than previously expected.

BUZZ Index April 2026 Rebalance Highlights

ImmunityBio Inc. (IBRX)

The largest addition to the BUZZ Index in April is a biotech company, ImmunityBio (IBRX). The company’s lead product, ANKTIVA, is a non-invasive immunotherapy for bladder cancer. Earlier this year, ImmunityBio released a series of positive headlines, including stronger-than-expected ANKTIVA sales, encouraging clinical trial results, and several regulatory approvals in international markets. At the same time, the FDA’s review of the company’s plans to expand ANKTIVA’s label appeared to be progressing well. This steady stream of positive developments helped fuel a sharp squeeze in the stock, sending shares higher by roughly 500% by the end of February. The story quickly gained traction across online platforms, with trading volumes surging as investors debated the company’s broader potential. Investor enthusiasm was further amplified by ambitious comments from Executive Chairman Patrick Soon-Shiong, who suggested ANKTIVA could function as a “cancer vaccine” for “all cancers”. However, that narrative was challenged in March when the FDA issued a warning letter regarding the claim, resulting in a sharp pullback in the stock. Despite the subsequent decline, the surge in investor sentiment and volume of conversation has propelled IBRX into the BUZZ Index this month at the maximum 3% weight.

Crypto-Related Equities

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) has retreated in recent months from its all-time highs, dragging down crypto-related companies ranging from digital asset treasuries (DATs), to miners, to digital transaction platforms. Despite this pullback, investor sentiment on the space has remained constructive over the past six months. Notably, this month saw a broad-based sentiment jump in crypto-related equities, resulting in four new crypto entrants into the BUZZ Index for April. The largest addition is CRCL (CRCL), the stablecoin issuer that went public last June, entering with a 1.08% weight. Close behind is TeraWulf (WULF), a clean-energy-focused Bitcoin miner, at 1.01%. BitMine Immersion Technologies (BMNR), a DAT positioning itself as the Ethereum version of Michael Saylor’s Strategy (MSTR), joins as a first-time constituent with a 0.95% weight. Finally, Galaxy Digital (GLXY) enters with a 0.66% weight. Founded by Mike Novogratz, GLXY operates a more institutionally oriented platform spanning trading, custody, staking, and asset management services. We view the sharp inflection in sentiment across crypto equities this month as notable and may signal an emerging turning point for the industry.

For more on rebalancing results and a full breakdown of index constituents added and removed for the month, view the BUZZ Index reconstitution report.

data.title: GE Vernova Q1: 75x Earnings And Still Not Expensive Enough
data.description:
I reiterate my Strong Buy rating on GE Vernova Inc. (GEV) after the stock price obliterated my $1,000 price target following a blowout Q1 2026 earnings report.

The quarter did more than beat the Street's estimates. What caught my eye in the report were several signs that suggest that the gas turbine and electrification supercycle is far from its peak.

In the power segment, the combined Gas Power backlog plus slot reservations increased from 83 GW to 100 GW in just one quarter, with management now expecting at least 110 GW by year-end.

On the electrification side, the company booked $2.4 billion of data-center-related equipment orders in Q1, more than all of 2025. I think this is enough proof for me to hold my position after the blowout quarter.

The wind segment is still under pressure, so I wouldn't discard more quarters like Q3 2025 ahead. However, I think the growth story of the two main segments (power and electrification) points to a higher price tag this year.

Q1 2026 - Another Blowout Quarter

By far, the item that caught my eye was the proof that the gas-turbine supercycle is still tightening, far from its peak.

In Q1, the combined Gas Power backlog (plus slot reservations) rose from 83 GW to 100 GW, and management now expects at least 110 GW by year-end 2026. On top of that, management said that the H1 2026 orders are being priced 10-20 points above Q4 '25 on a dollars-per-kilowatt basis. The outlook for the power segment in Q2 is looking at 15-17% organic revenue growth (vs. 10% in Q1). Notably, management expects an expansion to EBITDA margins as well, projecting 17-18% for Q2 vs. 16.3% in Q1.

To me, this is a strong sign that the gas power supercycle is far from its peak. That's essentially the main concern of the bears on this stock. Why?

Because of its grotesquely overvalued multiple. As I'm writing, the stock is trading at 75x forward earnings. At that valuation, any signs of a deterioration in the gas power cycle could lead to a double-digit drop.

That didn't happen after the last earnings release, as the stock is up over 10% after the print.

Now, the bull case on GE Vernova is not limited to electricity generation. As you may know, the company is perceived as a power infrastructure platform for the AI data center buildout, with exposure to both electricity generation and grid equipment.

Speaking of the latter, the Electrification segment booked $2.4 billion of Q1 equipment orders tied to data centers, more than all of 2025 (emphasis on this point), while segment backlog reached $42.4 billion, and segment EBITDA margin rose to 17.8%.

Speaking of backlog, the total backlog of the company reached $163 billion, and management pulled forward its expected path to $200 billion of backlog from 2028 to 2027.

For that reason, I expect analyst consensus for the next four years of sales to move up in the coming days.

So far, the current revisions suggest only a modest increase, although I think that is because analysts still need time to digest the release. Why?

Simply due to the high number of guidance upgrades:

Looking at the near term, the company raised its full-year 2026 revenue guidance to $44.5-$45.5 billion from $44-$45 billion in January.

Adjusted EBITDA margin was guided to 12%-14% from 11%-13%, and FCF to $6.5-$7.5 billion from $5.0-$5.5 billion.

All these metrics tell me one thing: the growth story is far from its peak. That said, there are a few risks worth keeping a close eye on. I discuss them below.

Risks

The Wind segment looks anything but compelling. This segment is getting hit from both sides. Let me explain.

Q1 wind revenue fell 23% YOY, and its EBITDA losses widened to $382 million.

As seen above, this segment, which accounted for approximately 15% of total revenue in Q1, is dragging down the company.

I wouldn't overlook this segment. Back in the October 2025 release, the stock was down 9% when GE Vernova warned that wind weakness would persist.

Is this priced in?

I truly don't know. I don't believe in the efficient market hypothesis, so I can't tell you how much of this headwind is baked into the price and how much isn't. All I know is that the market cares about this segment, given its double-digit contribution to total sales.

If that wasn't enough for the Wind segment, Q2 is guided to another mid-teens revenue decline with another $200 million-$300 million of losses. In fact, if one looks at the fine print of the FY26 guidance, management says that the guide assumes Wind organic revenue down low double digits and about $400 million of EBITDA losses.

In other words, the U.S. onshore order environment is still too uncertain to call a real inflection because of permitting delays and tariffs. Therefore, I would closely monitor any developments that may affect this segment.

Valuation

I love it when an outrageously expensive industrial stock keeps getting more expensive. The fact that the multiple is expanding is not a reason to rate this stock as a Sell, in my view.

Yes, I get it. Trading at 75x forward earnings when the industrial sector median is 20x is not an encouraging sign. However, the multiple expansion is justified, in my view, based on the fact that power appears to be a major reason behind possible delays in the AI data center buildout.

According to some sources, major data center projects, including buildouts from Microsoft (MSFT) and OpenAI (OPENAI), may miss their projected deadlines by more than three months. The report cites chronic shortages of labor, power, and equipment as the main reasons.

Well, it turns out that GE Vernova is exposed to two of the three factors. Just like we've seen the surge in memory stocks this year, I think this year will be the year of the power and equipment bottlenecks.

Looking Ahead

I see a regime where commercial intake is running materially ahead of factory and project revenue recognition. For reference, Q1 demand ran at about 1.97x the revenue conversion rate (side note: orders of $18.3 billion against revenue of $9.3 billion).

In fact, against the new $45.0 billion midpoint of 2026 revenue guidance, the $163.3 billion backlog at the end of Q1 equals roughly 3.63x one year of revenue.

Therefore, I don’t think we are nearing a peak cycle in either the power or electrification businesses. In fact, the Street expects an acceleration in revenue growth in the next 3 quarters.

Despite the drag of the Wind segment and the fact that there are no upside catalysts for this segment, I reiterate my Strong Buy on GE Vernova after spectacular results. As a side note, I have no price target in mind, given that the stock is at all-time highs after obliterating my $1,000 target earlier this year.

data.title: VantageScore 4.0 And AI Fundamentally Threaten Fair Isaac
data.description:
Introduction

The market has turned against a company famous for predictable cash flows, an incredible moat, and very reliable earnings growth. Having declined 55% since its peak in November of 2024, it is clear to the market that recent threats have changed its thesis. AI threatening its software offerings, as it has for other SaaS companies, and the FHFA’s approval of VantageScore 4.0 as an alternative to Fair Isaac Corporation (FICO) are two game-changing threats that appear to be the main drivers of this collapse. In this article I will analyze the threats and determine if the market is overreacting.

Its Moat

In the US, around 50% of home loans are sponsored by the government through Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, who buy mortgages and package them into MBS. To compare and securitize millions of mortgages, the market needed a uniform definition of borrower risk, and FICO, with its long historical track record, was perfectly positioned to become that standard. FICO had a data advantage through performance data across credit cycles and default correlation studies, which helped make the mortgage ecosystem build itself around FICO. MBS investor models, bank compliance frameworks, and risk-based pricing grids were all built around FICO, which made its moat exceptionally strong. With this moat, it enabled FICO to enjoy very high profitability metrics with a gross profit margin of 82%, an operating margin of 48%, a net margin of 31%, and an ROA of 35%.

Its high moat also allowed FICO to increase its mortgage score price by 8X since 2018. These price hikes show how indispensable FICO’s algorithms are and how much leverage FICO has historically had within the mortgage ecosystem.

With a perfect moat, high profitability, and growth, investors were willing to pay up to 100X earnings per share.

How These Two Things Threaten FICO

This moat is fundamentally challenged with the FHFA’s decision to approve VantageScore 4.0. The three major credit bureaus—Equifax, Experian, and TransUnion—collect raw consumer data from credit cards, loans, payment history, and delinquencies, which FICO then uses to build its scoring models. FICO builds its scoring models using data collected from the three main credit bureaus, which is then licensed back to the bureaus. The bureaus run the score models and sell the scores to lenders, which then use them in loan origination and delivery into the mortgage market. This is a very inefficient model that causes FICO to be the bridge between data and score for credit bureaus and the whole reason why VantageScore 4.0 is such a game changer. VantageScore 4.0 was created by a joint venture owned by the three major credit bureaus and has the potential to completely remove FICO from the equation. This more frictionless model allows VantageScore 4.0 to charge less; it currently costs $0.99, and TransUnion estimates the new pricing could drive more than $900 million in potential savings for lenders and consumers. This competitive pricing fundamentally threatens its moat and its high profitability that comes with it. I have seen articles saying that switching costs are too high to make a meaningful impact on FICO, but this is where the second threat comes into play: AI.

Just like AI is threatening SaaS companies, which is FICO's other business apart from its scoring segment, a similar thesis applies to FICO. Part of the reason why SaaS companies’ moat is threatened is because AI significantly lowers switching costs. Companies can use AI to migrate more easily from one software ecosystem to another. It is cheaper than ever for lenders to change their models to include VantageScore 4.0 or for investors to include it in their MBS models. As AI lowers migration costs, there may come a point where it is cheaper for companies to change their entire ecosystem than to keep paying five times more for a score. I believe its software service is also threatened by AI, as banks can build AI and machine learning tools faster and more adapted to their own needs. FICO’s analytical edge could become less relevant, and lower switching costs could also apply to these kinds of software products. At a 1.8% CAGR since 2019, it is not really the reason why investors are paying a massive premium; its software segment does not have the same moat.

The most worrying fact is that FICO continues to use debt, its revolver, and term loans to partially fund its aggressive share repurchase program. The whole reason why FICO has been able to allocate more cash to its share repurchase program than its free cash flow is that its source of cash is high margin, asset-light, and recurring. In FY25, FICO issued $1.5 billion in senior notes at 6%, and after its free cash flow, FICO still had an estimated buyback funding gap of about $880 million, which was effectively supported by leverage.

With a P/E of 38x, its earnings yield, assuming earnings stay flat, is 2.6%, which is lower than the roughly 4.74% after-tax cost of debt. This basically means management is taking on higher-cost debt to buy back a lower-yielding stock. In my opinion, this aggressive buyback program is entirely dependent on two things: that its EPS continue to grow at high double digits like it has before and that its business model continues to be high-margin, asset-light, and recurring, both of which are more uncertain than ever. While analysts say that management must have high conviction that it will be able to grow its EPS and that its business is not affected, I do not see FICO’s future as certain as before. With so many new factors in play, it looks like management is taking a significant gamble.

Risks To My Thesis

While I do believe that these are massive risks that will fundamentally threaten its business, FICO has also introduced many new features and services. FICO’s recent objective is to move most of its software products onto the FICO platform so it can sell more capabilities and expand through partners. Also, given that it is a massive risk for FICO that 51% of its revenue depends on the three credit bureaus, its mortgage-direct strategy through myFICO is an important diversification plan.

Its software, although growing slowly, has close to doubled its operating margin from 16% in 2021 to 30% in 2025. Because FICO’s software deals require custom workflows and integration into specific bank systems, it means they require a tailored service and higher personnel costs. This process can be significantly improved with AI, and as the CEO William Lansing said:

“We continue to invest in our software business. We're really bullish on it. It's growing really nicely. We do anticipate margin expansion because our new platform is built for scaling profitably”

Its bookings have increased 50% over the past year, and the significant changes to its platform mean margins may stay low in the short term. Everything points to significant margin improvements for its software segment.

Valuation

For the valuation, I assume that because FICO will not be able to increase score prices as aggressively as before, its score growth will decline from 22% to 5% in 2030. Also, for the same reason, I expect scores operating margin to decrease from 85% in 2026 to 50%, although it could be much lower. On the other hand, I expect its software growth to continue at similar rates as before (5%), and I expect its operating margin to increase from 30% to 45% by 2030. The weakest factor in my model is determining its share buyback program, as, in my opinion, its current, debt-funded one is not sustainable. Because FICO has historically had an aggressive share buyback program, I assume 80% of its FCF will continue to be deployed to buybacks. Also, historically its share buybacks have been offset by stock-based compensations.

For the WACC, I assumed that its cost of debt is 4.74% after tax, and for the cost of equity, I used CAPM. Because most of the weight comes from its cost of equity, I adjusted my WACC assumption from 9.85% to 8.85%.

Here are the DCF results:

It is clear that FICO is currently going through a massive change, as my assumptions have led me to calculate an intrinsic value between $400 and $600. This also shows that improvements in the software segment’s margins and growth are not enough to offset the massive changes the scores segment may go through. I use DCFs as a way to find large discrepancies in its current price, and this is a clear example. I believe I have pretty conservative assumptions, and still I see no positive indication.

Conclusion

FICO is currently in the unfortunate situation of not only being completely left out of the equation but also AI decreasing the switching costs to their lowest point ever. VantageScore 4.0 has many advantages, and it is aggressively trying to gain market share by charging 99 cents compared to FICO’s $5. In the long term, I see no way FICO will be able to charge less than VantageScore 4.0 with all the friction involved. I expect its EPS growth to slow down and its score margins to significantly decrease from its lucrative 85%. While I do see opportunities with its software segment and also by offering B2C scoring solutions, it is too early to know how these initiatives will play out. While I agree that adoption of VantageScore 4.0 across the industry may be slower than expected, I see that as only a short-term benefit for FICO. I think these risks are amplified by the fact that management still thinks it is a good decision to fund share buybacks with debt; this just sounds like a gamble. The valuation proved that these risks are detrimental to its price and outweigh its opportunities. I rate it a Strong Sell.

data.title: Palantir: AI No Longer A Differentiator
data.description:
When I last covered Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR) in March last year, I had a Hold rating, not because of uncertainty but due to its high valuation. Instead, while emphasizing its efficiency edge, I had shown that a better way to get exposure was through the Global X Defense Tech ETF (SHLD). Since then, the stock has appreciated by more than 75% and was trading around $146 at the time of writing.

This thesis aims to show that going forward, stock performance is likely to be more muted as the narrative around Palantir shifts from a near-monopolistic AI platform to one which is facing competition. Equally important, the Iran war has hinted that its artificial intelligence systems may not have performed optimally, as I discuss below through an analysis of its Maven AI platform.

The War May Have Shown the Limits of Maven AI

Palantir supports the U.S. military, including the Army, through its Maven Smart System. This AI platform was utilized during U.S. operations against Iran that began in late February 2026 as part of Operation Epic Fury.

Looking at the modus operandi, Maven ingests vast datasets from diverse sources like satellite imagery, drone feeds, radar data, and human-based reconnaissance to automatically detect potential targets. It also helps track threats (like missile attacks) and generate strike recommendations. Now, proceeding in such a data-driven way has significantly improved targets to hit, from days to minutes or even seconds, enabling over 1,000 strikes in the first 24 hours of combat. Eventually, more than 13,000 missions were carried out by American forces, which involved striking more than 12,000 targets aimed at slashing Iran’s ability to launch missiles and drones.

However, as shown by the oil facilities hit in the Middle East (both in Arab countries and Israel) by Iran more than one month after the start of operations, total U.S. air superiority remained elusive as the opponent retained its offensive capabilities, executed through drones and missiles. Worse, they managed to destroy a U.S. Air Force E-3 Sentry aircraft (with radar capabilities) on a Saudi Arabian air base.

Thinking aloud, this may show the limits of new automated AI gadgets versus more mature and tested technologies for targeting purposes. In this connection, after an Iranian school was accidentally bombed, AI was blamed for the missed targets. Now, there is also the human factor, as firstly, the targets have to be vetted by human operators, and secondly, an intelligence officer may have failed to update a database that the building was no longer used for military purposes. However, I believe that the fact that artificial intelligence is involved means questions are likely to be raised about AI-assisted targeting systems as Maven becomes an essential component of the American military.

Looking further, there are the economics as well, considering that an Iranian Shahed drone costs just $20K. In sharp contrast, the U.S. Army had to award a whopping $100 million contract to Palantir in 2024, just to expand the platform across all branches. Extending the comparison to interceptor missiles, for every false positive (erroneous target), a Tomahawk costing an average of $2 million goes to waste.

Consequently, the authorities may weigh in on the possibility of implementing cheaper AI, as enabled by AI startup Anthropic (ANTHRO), which demonstrated through its Cowork tool that it was possible to cost-effectively develop intelligent applications.

AI Likely to Impact the Commercial Business

To this end, in addition to software development, Cowork has advanced data collection from various sources and analysis (analytics) capabilities, two of the areas where Palantir specializes, as shown below, and which mostly account for the bulk of its product differentiation.

Hence, its products are used not only by the military but also by the commercial sector (banks, automakers, aircraft manufacturers, and food production, as shown below) to extract meaningful information out of raw data for decision-making.

Thus, Anthropic poses a threat to Palantir in the commercial sector because of its data management (synthesis of data across multiple sources, all done in an automated fashion) capabilities. The price action further supports this idea about the company being vulnerable to AI disruption threats, as the stock declined since Anthropic released Cowork at the end of January, as illustrated below. It was impacted to such a degree that, unlike SHLD, Palantir did not benefit from the Iran war, showing that the market probably thinks that disruption effects may offset war-related gains.

Discussing How Competition May Impact Palantir

Pursuing with a dose of realism, while Anthropic and OpenAI (OPENAI) are unlikely to displace Palantir’s central role in Maven anytime soon on their own, they can still eat into its market share by partnering with a defense startup that demonstrates superior capabilities when it comes to cost-effectively targeting threats.

Furthermore, to be fair, instead of penalizing Palantir because of Maven's potential limitations, the company could, on the contrary, benefit from additional upgrade contracts by the army to make the system more precise. Still, given AI disruption risks, the sales opportunities generated by the U.S. government may not be enough to offset lost opportunities in the commercial sector, which, despite forming less than half (47%) of total sales during the fourth quarter of 2025, grew much faster than the government segment (which includes defense), as shown below.

Arguing further, its platform, which possesses highly differentiated data analytics, faces competition as AI is democratized and other software vendors augment their product lines with Claude and similar tools, making Palantir's products more commoditized. This, in turn, implies that the company may potentially have to sell its products at lower contract values, reducing its pricing power. This, combined with higher R&D and marketing expenses (due to AI competition), may reduce the pace of operating margin growth and has to be considered when valuing the company.

The Multiple Could Come Down Further

Now, this is a company that was loss-making until 2022 but saw a turnaround in 2023 before seeing margins surge by roughly three times from FY-24 to FY-25, as charted below. This means that in case it is not able to continue showing progress, market sentiment may turn sour.

Furthermore, during my last coverage, it was trading at a forward GAAP P/E of 311x compared to 129x currently (or roughly a 58% drop), while the share price has increased by 75% with earnings also surging. In other words, the multiple has come down despite price gains, possibly indicating that profits (earnings) are catching up with that inflated multiple, a point that I had emphasized during my last coverage to justify getting exposure through the Global X ETF.

Looking deeper into the share price/valuation dynamics, this may be because of the emergence of AI competition from startups like Anthropic and OpenAI. As a result, the stock may now be increasingly perceived as a more “normal” IT company instead of boasting a monopolistic position. Going forward, I believe its P/E has more room to fall, but not all the way to the IT sector's 32x. Instead, due to its exposure to defense, a P/E of 85x, which is the multiple of Palo Alto (PANW), appears more appropriate. The AI disruption theme has also impacted this cybersecurity play, which provides mission-critical solutions to the commercial sector and the U.S. Government, and entails high switching costs.

A Hold in the Age of AI Disruption

In conclusion, this thesis has shown that as the narrative changes from a near-monopoly to one characterized by more competition, Palantir's profitability may suffer. This means it may not be able to deliver EPS at the accelerated pace expected, as shown below, because of a deceleration in margin expansion. This can cause EPS to grow more slowly than previously elevated expectations. This could trigger a multiple (P/E) compression, which would negatively impact the share price.

Therefore, it is not the time to buy, given that there is uncertainty related to Maven. In this case, Anthropic's Claude may have been used on top of the platform to augment the military's ability to rapidly identify targets in Iran, which may lead some to point out that Palantir is not to be blamed for the errors. Instead, the accusing finger may point to the AI start-up.

However, looking deeper, Claude does not function on a standalone basis but has been integrated (embedded) within the Maven ecosystem by Palantir (for prompt capabilities). Thus, accountability for errors could involve blame sharing involving Palantir. On top of that, the company could also be adversely impacted when strategists at the DoD reassess spending in light of the record $1.5 trillion military budget. In this connection, poor targeting led to the U.S. already spending nearly 45% of its precision strike missiles and around 50% of defense interceptors in the first seven weeks of the war.

Still, to be realistic, given Palantir's long track record, customers (including the government) are unlikely to instinctively switch to the most innovative AI startup, implying the company does not face the risk of experiencing an abrupt reduction in sales. On the other hand, even slight pricing pressures are likely to slow its phenomenal rate of margin expansion, leading to downgrade revisions by analysts. Therefore, I consider the stock to be a Hold, especially since the momentum points to a temporary rebound (price being above the 10-day and 50-day moving averages) but amid a longer-term downside (price under the 100-day and 200-day moving averages).

data.title: Commercial Real Estate: It's More Diverse Than You Think
data.description:
By Scott Greenfield and Brian Resnick, CFA

The opportunity set in this wide-ranging market extends well beyond office properties.

Investors often view commercial real estate (CRE) through the narrow lens of the office sector. We think this office-only focus understates how broad the asset class is and its potential. Offices may face well-documented headwinds, but many other CRE segments appear more resilient.

CRE spans a diverse range of asset types, including multifamily housing, industrial and logistics, hospitality, and niche sectors investors can access through both public and private markets. At a foundational level, we think CRE credit offers a distinct profile within fixed income. Loans are secured by buildings or properties that generate income and have their own value.

Unlike intellectual property or other intangible assets, we think real estate is less susceptible to being displaced by technology. Advances in artificial intelligence, for example, aren’t likely to stop people from living in apartment buildings or using warehouses for storage. In our view, that’s why much of this tangible collateral keeps its value - even in volatile markets.

CRE Deals Often Have Sizable Buffers for Lenders

The structural framework underpinning these investments is equally important. Real estate deals typically include substantial borrower equity that’s subordinate to the debt used to finance them. This equity creates a sizable buffer for credit investors in case the asset’s value declines.

These buffers are key, because, if property values decline enough, lenders take losses at a certain point. In the example below, the property’s value would have to decline by more than 30% before lenders would face losses (Display). This buttress enables credit investors to make generating income and having downside mitigation a priority instead of relying on the property value rising.

What’s more, we think commercial real estate debt’s combination of seniority, contractual income streams and physical collateral can foster durability across economic cycles. For investors, that resilience can shift the emphasis away from trying to time the real estate market. In our view, it also helps explain the historical staying power of CRE credit, even in uncertain environments.

A Transition, Not Decline, for Commercial Real Estate

We think the CRE landscape is in a period of transition - not broad-based contraction. Capital markets have recovered from their 2025 lows, thanks in part to increased participation from private credit providers, insurance investors and structured-products markets.

And while many office properties face challenges from changing work habits and what employers and employees want from a modern office, that doesn’t add up to broad-based decline. Asset quality and location matter. What’s more, developers are increasingly adapting unwanted offices for other purposes.

In sectors beyond office space, the CRE picture is brighter. Demand for industrial property remains stable, while high-quality hotels and resorts continue to outperform. Meanwhile, high real estate prices and limited supply are boosting demand for multifamily residential real estate, brightening that sector’s medium-term outlook. Slower home construction activity is likely to keep a lid on excess inventory (Display), and support home prices and rental values - even in a more volatile interest-rate environment.

Convergence Underway Across Public, Private Markets

Investors have historically accessed CRE credit exposure through either private lending or public market vehicles such as commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS). Increasingly, private and public channels are converging, creating a more flexible and dynamic investment landscape.

In private markets, investors can originate or provide capital directly to borrowers, often capturing a premium for sourcing, loan structuring and illiquidity. These investments typically offer structural features and attractive income profiles that may reduce mark-to-market volatility.

Public markets can present compelling opportunities during periods of dislocation. As we see it, recent volatility has been driven more by macro uncertainty than by deteriorating fundamentals. That disruption could create attractive entry points for investors.

Single Asset Single Borrower: Best of Bold Worlds?

Positioned between public and private segments is the growing single asset single borrower (SASB) market, where transactions have elements of both public and private investment, including:

  • Access to institutional assets, either individually or through a diversified portfolio
  • Simpler loan structures than the more tailored structures in private markets
  • Liquidity and price transparency associated with public securitized markets

With the potential to combine asset-level underwriting discipline with market-based liquidity, we think SASB deals broaden investors’ toolkits.

Rather than viewing public and private markets as siloes, portfolios can allocate dynamically based on relative value. This may mean emphasizing private lending when spreads are lower and leaning into public markets when volatility creates price dislocations. With SASB structures, meanwhile, investors can access high-quality assets with attractive structures and liquidity.

A Diverse Opportunity Set

Commercial real estate isn’t monolithic, and it’s not defined by the office sector alone. It’s a diverse asset class that spans public and private markets, offering exposure to income-generating physical assets with conservative capital structures that may enhance risk-mitigation potential.

For investors, we think the commercial real estate market adds up to a compelling set of opportunities. Periods of uncertainty can obscure these dynamics. But these are often the moments when relative-value dynamics are most attractive.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

data.title: Tesla's Terafab Dream
data.description:
Tesla (TSLA) had their earnings yesterday, but given the company trades at ~226x NTM EV/EBIT multiple, it is a bit of a waste of time to spend time dissecting their existing business. Ultimately, Tesla has become a moonshot bet on several projects that currently do not contribute materially to their numbers. Full Self Driving, or FSD, Robotaxi, Optimus, and more recently, Terafab are some of their key moonshot projects. It’s not often that I come across companies in public markets which are generously valued purely based on optional value attached to ongoing projects with a high degree of uncertainty attached to it, so it speaks volumes of the level of confidence investors have shown on Elon Musk’s ability to unlock value even from what it may appear to be pretty improbable scenarios.

Even by Tesla’s standards, the Terafab project is perhaps the epitome of Musk’s eternal desire to swing for the fences. So, what exactly is this project about?

Terafab is a planned ~$25 billion semiconductor fab that Elon Musk announced last month as a joint venture between Tesla, SpaceX (SPACE), and xAI (X.AI). The name comes from its headline target: 1 terawatt of annual compute output, which Musk mentioned to be ~50x what all existing global fabs produce today combined. It will target 2nm process technology and an initial capacity of 100,000 wafer starts per month. Almost every aspect of this project screams of a quixotic dream, so I was curious to hear more about Terafab project in yesterday’s call. When asked about Terafab, Elon Musk said the following:

“we’re still working out the details of the Terafab deployment. In the near term, Tesla will be building the research fab on our Giga Texas campus. This is something we expect to be probably a $3 billion-ish initiative and capable of maybe a few thousand wafers per month, but it’s really intended to try out ideas. The research fab, it was in terms of maybe -- we have some ideas for improving the fundamental technology of how chips are made and some new physics we’d like to test out, but we also want to test out the ability to -- to see if something is working in production. So you need kind of like a few thousand wafer starts a month to make sure that a production process is sound.

And then SpaceX is going to take care of like the initial phase of the scaled up Terafab. And that’s what we figured out thus far. I think this will be unique in the world, or at least I’m not aware of any a place where you have the lithography mask creation, and then logic, memory and packaging in under one roof in one building. That’s about the fastest I could possibly imagine doing [ recourse ] of research and development and being able to try out some pretty radical ideas, some of which have -- it’s kind of long-shot stuff, but if some of these long shots pan out, it would be radical improvements in the way [indiscernible] work.”

Indeed, that does sound like a pretty long shot! A conventional leading-edge fab actually handles only wafer fabrication and ASML (ASML) provides the lithography tools. Design happens at fabless customers, memory is made at separate fabs (Micron (MU), Samsung (SSNLF), SK hynix), and advanced packaging and test are typically done at OSAT facilities or dedicated packaging sites. Terafab's claim is that design, lithography, fabrication, memory production, advanced packaging, and testing all happen at a single site. My guess is that anyone other than Elon Musk would not dare propose something like this. But once you've figured out how to catch a returning rocket booster with a pair of mechanical arms, the word "impossible" probably stops meaning what it means to the rest of us. If anything, such consensus of incredulity may have only propelled him to give this a shot. We don’t need to believe this project has any credible path when Musk himself acknowledges the long-shot nature of the bet, but admittedly I’m glad that he’s giving this a go without accepting the “fate” that the semi value chain has reached almost the end state.

To be clear, Musk isn’t doing this because he’s annoyed by the ever-burgeoning profit pool of the players in the semi value chain, but because he thinks the compute need is going to be so great that it would be pretty much impossible to meet demand with the existing ecosystem. From the call yesterday:

Terafab is not some sort of mechanism to generate leverage over our chip suppliers. It's just literally, we don't see a path to having enough efficient quantity of AI chips down the road. As we scale production to high levels, just the rate at which the industry is growing in logic, but even more so in memory, it's just doesn't -- we just anticipate hitting a wall if we don't make chips ourselves.

As a reminder, TSMC (TSM) was also asked about Tesla’s Terafab project during their earnings call. They didn’t sound concerned. From the call (emphasis mine):

“…both Intel (INTC) and Tesla, they are TSMC’s customers. But again, they are our competitors, and we view Intel as our formidable competitor and do not underestimate them. But having said that, there are no shortcuts. The fundamental rules of the foundry game never change. They need the technology leadership, manufacturing excellence and customer trust, and most of all, the service, which has been mentioned by Jensen; thank you for his wording.

Again, let me say that it takes 2 to 3 years to build a new fab, no shortcuts. And it takes another 1 to 2 years to ramp it up. Again, that’s the fundamental of foundry industry. And whether we try to win them back, actually, they are still our customer. And we are very confident in our technology position, and we work very hard to capture every piece of business possible.

I will continue to listen to Tesla’s earnings calls to stay updated on their moonshot projects, especially Terafab.

Original Post
